children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation – Mandates and background

18.1	The Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) is responsible for promoting the economic and social development of its members, fostering intraregional integration and promoting international cooperation for the development of Africa. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, and Economic and Social Council resolution 671 A (XXV).

18.2	ECA provides innovative solutions, together with demand-driven and tailor-made policy and technical advice, grounded in evidence, to its members and regional economic communities to strengthen their social and economic resilience and achieve the goals of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and Agenda 2063: The Africa We Want, of the African Union. ECA is well positioned to support member States, within its mandate, in the implementation of relevant commitments and actions outlined in the Pact for the Future (General Assembly resolution 79/1). ECA also works to build the capacity of its members to formulate and implement policies for sustainable development, including through the implementation of the regular programme for technical cooperation and Development Account projects.
Strategy and external factors for 2027
18.3	In 2027, ECA will concentrate on five strategic directions:

(a)	Deploying knowledge to support policymaking to drive the development agenda of Africa and foster infrastructure development, including in the areas of energy; technology, innovation and connectivity; and climate action and food security;
(b)	Formulating policy options, including macroeconomic policies, to accelerate sustainable industrialization and economic diversification;
(c)	Enhancing the mainstreaming of data and statistics, designing and implementing financing models and leveraging them for the development of human, physical and social infrastructure assets;

(d)	Supporting deeper regional integration and the development of regional public goods, focusing on social inclusion and considering synergies between the economic and social development programmes of Africa;
(e)	Advocating a common position for Africa at the global level and developing regional responses as a contribution to global governance issues.

18.4	Within its existing mandate, and in line with the actions and decisions contained in the Pact for the Future and the commitments contained in the Global Digital Compact and the Declaration on Future Generations, ECA will pay particular attention to supporting member States in leveraging technology and artificial intelligence for economic and social development and promoting digital cooperation, as well as further developing sustainable regional value chains for economic diversification, harnessing the demographic dividend and increasing engagement with young people in the design of policies and programmes.

18.5	In pursuing these strategic directions, ECA will employ its convening function, through the provision of multilateral and multi-stakeholder platforms such as the Africa Regional Forum on Sustainable Development, the Conference of African Ministers of Finance, Planning and Economic Development, and the subregional intergovernmental committees of experts and senior officials; its think tank function, by conducting interdisciplinary research and the analysis of key challenges facing its members and Africa as a whole, while promoting peer learning and development; and its operational function, through the provision of tailor-made policy and technical advice to its members, including in cooperation with other entities of the United Nations system. In line with action 45 (a) of the Pact for the Future, ECA will further strengthen its institutional capabilities to better support member States in delivering on its mandates. The work of ECA will additionally be anchored in other multilateral frameworks such as the Paris Agreement, the Sevilla Commitment of the Fourth International Conference on Financing for Development and the Doha Political Declaration of the World Social Summit.

18.6	With a view to enabling a sharper focus on priorities for the continent and to better reflect mandates entrusted to ECA by the Conference of African Ministers of Finance, Planning and Economic Development, subprogramme 6 has been renamed “Economic empowerment of women and youth” to reflect the support provided by the subprogramme on issues related to young people. In addition, subprogramme 9 has been renamed “Poverty and socioeconomic policy” to better reflect the focus of the subprogramme’s work. These changes were endorsed by the Conference of African Ministers in its report (E/ECA/COE/44/23).

18.7	With regard to cooperation with other entities, ECA will continue its close cooperation with the African Union Commission and will build upon its existing strategic partnerships with regional and subregional organizations, central banks, universities, think tanks and civil society and private sector organizations and forge new partnerships towards the fulfilment of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063. Regarding South-South and triangular cooperation, ECA will leverage partnerships premised on the principle of enhancing complementarity and impact.

18.8	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, ECA, as Co-Chair, will continue to work through the Regional Collaborative Platform for Africa and its opportunity and issue-based coalitions, and will enhance collaboration with regional United Nations entities, the Department of Economic and Social Affairs and the United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD). At the country level, ECA will work closely with resident coordinators and country teams on projects to respond to country demands.
18.9	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Members of ECA and regional economic communities will actively engage with the recommended policies, models, tools, norms and standards of ECA, prioritizing their adoption and integration, and will continue to cooperate among and between themselves for harmonized action towards the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063;

(b)	The Africa Regional Forum on Sustainable Development, the Conference of African Ministers of Finance, Planning and Economic Development, and the subregional intergovernmental committees of experts and senior officials continue to provide policy advice to ECA on emerging priorities.

18.10	ECA integrates a gender perspective in its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate, by promoting further alignment and coherence between global, regional and national frameworks on gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls. Moreover, subprogramme 6 will continue to support members of ECA in achieving Sustainable Development Goal 5 and will provide technical support to the Commission’s other subprogrammes to integrate a gender perspective into their work.

18.11	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, ECA will pursue the implementation of measures to ensure the inclusion of persons with disabilities in its operational activities, programmes and projects, as appropriate, including the provision of reasonable accommodation for conference and workshop participants.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

18.12	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. For example, the urbanization workstream, previously under subprogramme 9, has been integrated into subprogramme 1, as reflected in the updated strategies and deliverables of both subprogrammes.

18.13	ECA will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals for strengthening collaboration with UNCTAD, the Department of Economic and Social Affairs and the other regional commissions through joint mechanisms. ECA will jointly explore options for strengthening macroeconomic analysis capacities, with a view to contributing more harmonized analysis and policy support to Member States. ECA will also consider proposals aimed at streamlining support for the least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States and strengthening and optimizing organizational arrangements in this regard, as appropriate.

children.text: Evaluation activities

18.16	Eight project evaluations conducted by ECA and completed in 2025, in the areas of African Continental Free Trade Area implementation, industrialization, climate-resilient development pathways, and migration, have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027.

18.17	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, ECA will integrate lessons learned and recommendations to inform the implementation and the design of the next phases of the projects. For example, projects to operationalize the African Continental Free Trade Area will strengthen its communication and outreach strategies to ensure a more targeted approach, with a view to fostering deeper stakeholder engagement and effective uptake of trade preferences and instruments. In addition, ECA will strive to enhance focus areas of project interventions to leverage its comparative advantage and avoid duplication of interventions at the country level.
18.18	The following evaluations to be conducted by ECA are planned for 2027:
(a)	Five project evaluations on ECA support in the areas of trade, public finance, climate finance, and digitalization of civil registration and vital statistics systems;
(b)	Programme evaluation on digitalization and data policies in Africa.
Subprogramme 1 – Macroeconomic policy, finance and governance
Objective

18.19	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to achieve structural transformation and inclusive growth in Africa through strengthened and effective development planning, macroeconomic policy analysis, enhanced economic governance and management, and innovative public and private sector financing.
Strategy
18.20	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Provide technical assistance, capacity-building and policy advice to members of ECA on:
(i)	Tracking progress in the implementation of the 2030 Agenda, Agenda 2063 and the Doha Programme of Action, and designing, implementing and monitoring their national development plans;

(ii)	Designing and implementing policy frameworks that promote transparency and accountability in public financial management, including debt management, and resource mobilization and allocation to build resilience to shocks;
(iii)	Identifying priority growth sectors and promoting the development and implementation of initiatives to improve their competitiveness, attract investment and stimulate entrepreneurship;
(iv)	Developing and deepening their domestic capital markets and developing innovative and sustainable financing solutions for mobilizing additional resources to meet development financing needs;

(v)	Enhancing the credibility, transparency and resilience of African economies through improved sovereign credit ratings, thereby facilitating access to affordable financing through better risk perception;
(vi)	Promoting sustainable urbanization that enhances job creation, revenues and urban social protection.

(b)	Convene platforms for policy dialogue on matters related to macroeconomic policy, economic governance, financing and development planning, including the annual session of the Conference of African Ministers of Finance, Planning and Economic Development and the biennial session of the Committee on Economic Governance;

(c)	Publish policy-relevant knowledge products underpinned by research and macroeconomic modelling tools and produce, upon request, forecasts with recommendations for responding to the impacts of external shocks.
18.21	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	More coordinated and integrated policy design and implementation by members of ECA;
(b)	Accelerated and sustainable graduation of African countries from the least developed country category;
(c)	Increased mobilization of resources and capital for development and investment in priority areas;
(d)	Improved debt management and reduced debt vulnerabilities among members of ECA;
(e)	An enabling environment that allows for improved access to markets and a diversified investor base with an increased appetite for domestic currency debt markets;
(f)	Improved borrowing conditions and greater fiscal space to drive sustainable economic and social development;
(g)	Improved economic and financial resilience in African cities.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened debt management and sustainable financing strategies in small island developing States

18.22	Multiple global crises continue to severely impact developing countries, particularly small island developing States, by disrupting their economic activities and value chains, further exacerbating their fiscal vulnerabilities. The subprogramme provided support to Cabo Verde and the Comoros to address heightened fiscal constraints, compounded by limited access to external financing for their development priorities. The intervention aimed to strengthen debt management and external financing frameworks by enhancing the capacity of the two countries in designing their financing strategies. The subprogramme, in collaboration with UNCTAD, conducted debt sustainability analyses, risk and resilience assessments related to the impacts of climate change, and external financing diagnostics, to identify practical pathways to mobilize sustainable and affordable external financing while containing debt vulnerabilities in the countries. This led to the development of national financing strategies and corresponding implementation road maps to guide implementation in Cabo Verde and the Comoros.
18.23	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.2).

children.text: Result 2: enhanced macroeconomic forecasting and development planning for inclusive and sustainable growth – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.26	The subprogramme’s work contributed to strengthened national capacity for macroeconomic forecasting and evidence-based planning in Botswana, which did not meet the planned target of one additional country applying macroeconomic analysis tools in the development of national development plans. The target was not met due to the need for further consultations with additional line ministries in the preparation of the national development plan.

Figure 18.I
Performance measure: number of members of ECA applying macroeconomic analysis tools in the development of national development plans (cumulative)
Result 3: improved economic and financial governance to enhance structural transformation and inclusive development – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.28	Effective economic and financial governance strengthens domestic resource mobilization, creates a conducive environment for investment, and directs resources effectively to foster inclusive development and structural transformation. The subprogramme provided technical support to members of ECA to improve their tax administration, including the rationalization of tax expenditures, tackling profit shifting, and enhancing resource mobilization. In addition, support was provided in developing sovereign credit rating frameworks aligned with international standards to better prepare for effective engagement with credit rating agencies.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.29	The lesson for the subprogramme was the need to support members of ECA to develop robust governance frameworks to improve credit ratings and tax administration systems. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will provide technical support and capacity-building to members of ECA on strengthening their governance frameworks to improve their domestic resource mobilization and sovereign credit rating.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Regional integration and trade
Objective

18.32	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen regional cooperation and integration among members of ECA through increased trade flows, improved industrialization and increased investment.
Strategy
18.33	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide advisory services and technical assistance to members of ECA on market access and business opportunities arising from the African Continental Free Trade Area to minimize potential adverse effects (such as import surges, dumping and customs revenue loss risks) in the context of the implementation of their national strategies, and multilateral and bilateral trade issues;

(b)	Develop and disseminate knowledge products and provide technical assistance to identify opportunities for diversification, value addition and the development of manufacturing and industrial clusters for members of ECA in support of the Action Plan for the Accelerated Industrial Development of Africa, contributing to the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 9 and 17;

(c)	Provide training, with a particular focus on least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and regional economic communities, on the implementation of existing regional cooperation frameworks;

(d)	Provide advisory services and technical assistance to members of ECA and regional economic communities on regional integration, national, regional and continental competition policies, intellectual property policies and measures for investment facilitation.
18.34	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved implementation by countries of their respective strategies for the operationalization of the African Continental Free Trade Area;
(b)	More coherent, coordinated and responsive interfaces between the African Continental Free Trade Area and the free trade areas and customs unions of the regional economic communities;
(c)	More coherent trade and industrialization policies aligned with the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063;
(d)	Accelerated industrialization and increased diversification of productive capacities and production patterns;
(e)	Reduced transaction risks and intermediation costs for members of ECA;

(f)	An improved business environment characterized by enhanced opportunities for private sector operators to leverage transboundary opportunities and improved market access conditions for African trade of goods and services, as well as the free movement of capital and people.
Programme performance in 2025
Enhanced readiness of Ethiopia to trade under the African Continental Free Trade Area

18.35	Ethiopia’s effective participation in the African Continental Free Trade Area is a strategic priority for accelerating export diversification, regional value chain integration, and private sector competitiveness. As a late entrant to preferential trade arrangements, Ethiopia faces specific structural, regulatory and institutional constraints that would need to be addressed to translate African Continental Free Trade Area commitments into tangible trading outcomes for firms, particularly small and medium-sized enterprises. To address these gaps, the subprogramme supported Ethiopia in strengthening its operational readiness to trade under the African Continental Free Trade Area by addressing policy, regulatory and implementation bottlenecks across goods and services trade. Key interventions focused on enhancing national coordination mechanisms, strengthening the understanding of the African Continental Free Trade Area among public and private stakeholders, and supporting evidence-based engagement in continental negotiations. Technical assistance and capacity-building activities helped national institutions to align domestic trade policies, customs procedures, and trade facilitation frameworks with African Continental Free Trade Area requirements, while targeted analytical work supported Ethiopia’s positioning in priority sectors with regional export potential. These interventions contributed to improved institutional preparedness and clearer national positions on African Continental Free Trade Area implementation and strengthened the capacity of Ethiopian trade officials and private sector actors to utilize African Continental Free Trade Area preferences effectively.
18.36	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.6).
Table 18.6
Performance measure

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: sustainable trade strategies to mitigate the impact of climate change – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.37	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the development of sustainable trade strategies that include considerations related to climate change in five countries, which did not meet the planned target of five additional members of ECA adopting sustainable trade strategies that include considerations related to climate change. The target was not met due to the need for further data collection to finalize the strategies.
18.38	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.II).
Figure 18.II
Performance measure: number of members of ECA adopting sustainable trade strategies that include considerations related to climate change (cumulative)
Result 2: members of ECA adopt measures to de-risk investments in regional value chains and special economic zones – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.39	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the development and validation of an evidence-based framework on standards and norms for successful special economic zones and regional value chains, which did not meet the planned target of two ECA member States adopting measures to de-risk investments in regional value chains and special economic zones. The target was not met because of the depth of the analytical work and focus on generating robust evidence and consensus-building among key stakeholders.
18.40	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.III).
Figure 18.III
Performance measure: number of ECA member States adopting measures to de-risk investments in regional value chains and special economic zones (annual)

Result 3: improved national policy and institutional environment for the private sector to participate in intra-African trade through the African Continental Free Trade Area – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.41	Effective participation in the African Continental Free Trade Area and related trade reforms requires sustained institutional readiness and negotiation capacity at the national and regional levels. Although many member States and regional economic communities have made progress in aligning policies with African Continental Free Trade Area commitments, disparities persist in their ability to negotiate, articulate national and regional interests, and consistently operationalize agreed trade rules. These gaps affect the depth and effectiveness of participation in trade processes and limit the realization of integration gains.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.42	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, while analytical products, policy frameworks and regional-level engagements remain essential, their impact on observable change was greatest when complemented by more targeted, country-level and regional economic community-specific approaches. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will place greater emphasis on approaches tailored to the institutional readiness of beneficiaries, with increased focus on country- and regional economic community-level application of trade rules, negotiation scenarios and operational decision-making. Building on progress achieved in previous periods, the subprogramme will support members of ECA by increasing awareness of African Continental Free Trade Area provisions and providing tailored technical assistance towards institutional alignment with the African Continental Free Trade Area and strengthening policy frameworks through practical step-by-step trade guides.
18.43	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.IV).

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Technology, innovation, connectivity and infrastructure development
Objective

18.45	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance inclusive growth, sustainable development and economic transformation in Africa through science, technology and innovation, improved connectivity, logistics and infrastructure development.
Strategy
18.46	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Conduct policy research and analysis on:
(i)	Digital trends on the continent, including those related to the economic impact of new and emerging technologies and technology-driven solutions for sustainable development;

(ii)	The state of energy, transport, and information and communications technology (ICT) infrastructure at the national and regional levels and the impact of logistics and connectivity on economic development;
(b)	Provide technical assistance and advisory services to members of ECA, the African Union and its bodies, and subregional organizations on:

(i)	The design and implementation of regulatory frameworks that promote infrastructure, digital systems and policies related to technology and innovation, including principles for good digital identification, data governance, ICT security, financial technology and responsible use of artificial intelligence;
(ii)	The design and implementation of policies and strategies to bridge technological and innovation gaps and promote digital inclusivity and digital connectivity across the continent;

(iii)	The development of an academic and entrepreneurial ecosystem for science, technology and innovation through partnerships and capacity-building initiatives with a view to developing a critical mass of researchers, experts and innovators for evidence-based decision-making and innovation-driven growth in member States;
(iv)	The implementation of the Programme for Infrastructure Development in Africa and the design of regulatory frameworks to enhance private sector investment in infrastructure while managing risk;

(v)	The development of transport infrastructure and services, including transport facilitation, norms and standards, regulations, road safety and asset management, and a particular focus on the liberalization of intra-African air transport in the context of the Single African Air Transport Market and the digitalization of transport infrastructure;
(vi)	The development of infrastructure to improve energy access, advance market development, and foster the transition to renewable sources of energy.
18.47	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Improved digital ecosystems across the continent, enabling the implementation of better policy and regulatory frameworks that enhance the use of digital public infrastructure, innovation and digital systems;

(b)	Strengthened and enhanced digital inclusivity and connectivity in members of ECA and across the continent, through effective policies and strategies, reducing technological disparities and ensuring broader access to digital resources and innovations;

(c)	A strengthened science, technology and innovation ecosystem with a greater number of capacity-building initiatives, leading to a critical mass of researchers, experts and innovators driving evidence-based decision-making and innovation-driven growth;
(d)	Increased efficiency and safety of the African transport sector;
(e)	Increased energy access and innovative financing for energy infrastructure;
(f)	Increased private sector investment in new and emerging technologies and public infrastructure.
18.48	In doing so, the subprogramme will support members of ECA in making progress towards Sustainable Development Goals 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 13 and 17.
Programme performance in 2025
Member States have access to a tax policy simulation tool to support evidence-based policymaking for affordable connectivity, inclusive economic growth, and job creation in Africa

18.49	Digital connectivity is a key driver of economic growth, employment and social inclusion in Africa. However, many individuals and businesses still face barriers due to high access costs (continental average of 14.8 per cent of gross national income per capita, nearly five times the global average) and limited broadband investments. Cognizant of these challenges, the subprogramme developed a technical report on optimizing Africa’s ICT tax regimes towards economic growth and job creation to inform policymakers of the economic and fiscal impacts of ICT taxation and to provide evidence-based recommendations for digital transformation. Key findings from the subprogramme’s research include the finding that a 10 per cent increase in mobile broadband penetration yields growth in gross domestic product by approximately 2.5 per cent and in employment rates between 3.1 per cent and 13.2 per cent. In addition, the subprogramme developed the ICT Tax Impact Calculator, a practical policy tool that enables Governments to simulate country-specific ICT tax reform scenarios and assess their potential economic and fiscal impacts. In 2025, the tool was presented and validated to demonstrate that the reduction of sector-specific excise duties can boost aggregate tax receipts by expanding the tax base, with a view to supporting the adoption of evidence-based fiscal policies that promote digital inclusion, revenue generation and job creation.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened strategies and policies for infrastructure development – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.55	Infrastructure development is a key driver of sustainable development on the continent and a critical enabler for trade, economic growth and regional integration. Research conducted by ECA indicates that the continent’s infrastructure deficit reduces economic growth by 2 per cent annually and cuts productivity by up to 40 per cent. The subprogramme supports member States and regional organizations through technical assistance, capacity-building, and knowledge-sharing in infrastructure development, with a view to accelerating structural transformation, promote innovation-driven growth and foster regional integration.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.56	The lesson for the subprogramme was the need to provide more targeted support at the regional and country levels for the development of comprehensive infrastructure strategies, including through a stronger focus on financing approaches, given that access to adequate financing remains a key challenge. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will strengthen its support to members of ECA and regional entities in the development and validation of infrastructure strategies, including by expanding tailored advisory services, promoting innovative and sustainable financing solutions, and deepening engagement with national stakeholders and United Nations country teams.

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Data and statistics
Objective

18.59	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen the production, dissemination and use of credible data, statistics and geospatial information at the national, regional and global levels for evidence-based policy and decision-making.
Strategy
18.60	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support African national statistical systems in transforming their data governance and management methods by digitalizing the data collection, compilation and dissemination process for statistical areas, including censuses and civil registration systems, and provide technical assistance and training in the design and implementation of national strategies for the development of statistics and national spatial data infrastructure with related integrated geospatial information frameworks;

(b)	Maintain and regularly update databases that are accessible for public use, with available harmonized data, statistics and geospatial information disaggregated by location on such topics as African economies, demographics, social indicators, the environment and other statistics needed for monitoring and reporting on the implementation of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063;
(c)	Provide training and make platforms available for the exchange of best practices among members of ECA and their respective statistical agencies;

(d)	Integrate policy advice and sustained capacity-building activities on data, statistics and geospatial information to support members of ECA in enhancing monitoring and reporting on the implementation of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063;
(e)	Enhance communication and advocacy to members of ECA on the use of statistics and geospatial information;

(f)	Enhance collaboration with policymakers and key data users to co-create innovative, user-oriented and digitally enabled statistical products that improve accessibility and strengthen their use in evidence-based policymaking.
18.61	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Improved capacity, resilience and efficiency of national statistical systems, including the uptake of innovative tools and technologies to collect comparable statistics at various levels of disaggregation, and improved statistical operations, including the conduct of censuses and surveys and administrative data processing and analysis, and dissemination of timely data;

(b)	Improved uptake of geospatial information management, closer collaboration between components and stakeholders of the national spatial data infrastructures, and better integrated geospatial information frameworks;
(c)	Enhanced monitoring and reporting on the implementation of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063;

(d)	Increased availability and uptake of innovative, user-oriented and digitally enabled statistical products, leading to improved accessibility and more effective use of official statistics in evidence-based policymaking.
Programme performance in 2025
Members of ECA produce and use improved economic statistics for regional integration policies

18.62	Improving the integration and consistency of key economic data sets covering production statistics, supply and use frameworks, input-output structures and trade data enables more robust measurement of production structures and trade flows. The subprogramme strengthened the capacities of members of ECA and regional institutions to produce and analyse core economic statistics relevant to trade and regional integration, including the development of semi-automated routines to integrate and generate supply and use tables, input-output tables and trade in value added indicators, which together form a structured framework for analysing economic interconnectedness. Ten African countries are now integrated into the African Continental Input-Output Table, with trade in value added indicators generated, thereby enhancing their capacity to formulate more accurate regional integration policies.
18.63	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.V).
Figure 18.V – Performance measure: number of members of ECA producing data integrated into the continental input-output table and trade in value added framework (annual)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: progress towards integrated statistical and geospatial information frameworks – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.64	The subprogramme’s work contributed to the application of the strategic pathways of the United Nations Integrated Geospatial Information Framework by three additional members of ECA (Eritrea, Ghana and Namibia), which met the planned target.
18.65	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.VI).
Figure 18.VI – Performance measure: number of members of ECA applying at least one of the strategic pathways of the United Nations Integrated Geospatial Information Framework (cumulative)
Result 2: improved civil registration and vital statistics systems – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.66	The subprogramme’s work contributed to strengthened capacity of four members of ECA (Burundi, Kenya, Namibia and United Republic of Tanzania) in civil registration and vital statistics pipeline applications, which did not meet the planned target of three members of ECA adopting innovative technology applications. The target was not met due to the need for further training prior to the adoption of the applications.
18.67	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.VII).
Figure 18.VII – Performance measure: number of members of ECA adopting innovative technology applications in civil registration and vital statistics systems (cumulative)

Result 3: strengthened national statistical systems enabling the regular production and policy use of innovative statistical products for evidence-based decision-making – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.68	Innovative user-centred statistical products are essential for strengthening evidence-based policymaking, monitoring national development plans, and advancing progress towards the Sustainable Development Goals and Agenda 2063. Despite improved statistical production, many ECA member States still struggle to transform existing data into timely, accessible and policy-relevant products that meet decision makers’ needs. The subprogramme has supported progress by helping countries enhance data dissemination, strengthen producer-user engagement and develop digital platforms for accessing official statistics, including census and survey result trackers, monitoring dashboards, and communication and project management toolkits.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.69	The lesson for the subprogramme was that statistical products have greater policy impact when they respond directly to clearly defined policy questions and are developed through early, continuous engagement with policymakers and other key users. Experience also showed that innovation is sustainable only when countries establish feedback mechanisms and have the capacity to iteratively update products using digital tools. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will strengthen co-creation with policymakers and end users; intensify support for developing digital, interactive and geospatially enabled statistical products; and help Member States institutionalize feedback systems for continuous improvement. This will enable countries to produce statistical products that remain policy-relevant, widely accessible and regularly used in decision-making processes.
18.70	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.VIII).
Figure 18.VIII – Performance measure: number of statistical products co-developed and in use by members of ECA for policy formulation and monitoring (cumulative)

children.text: Subprogramme 5 – Climate change, food security and natural resources
Objective

18.72	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to enhance climate resilience, achieve food security, and advance inclusive and sustainable development by harnessing land, natural resources, and the green and blue economies.
Strategy
18.73	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Conduct policy research and analysis on:

(i)	Mineral-led production linkages and the design and implementation of mineral policies, strategies and plans that consider economic, environmental and social aspects and matters related to gender equality, in line with the principles of the Africa Mining Vision;

(ii)	The circular economy, the development of the green economy in the context of sustainable development and poverty eradication, including the conduct of voluntary national and local reviews, and the blue economy, in line with the Africa Blue Economy Strategy of the African Union;

(iii)	Strengthening resilience to climate change and reducing disaster vulnerability of African economies, societies, ecosystems and agrifood systems through integrated implementation of climate action;
(iv)	Modelling of the economic impacts of climate change to support the implementation of nationally determined contributions;

(v)	Land governance, including policy and regulatory reforms for land tenure security, in particular for women and young people, and the use of the Framework and Guidelines on Land Policy in Africa and geographic information system-based tools to support decision-making regarding responsible land-based investments by the private sector;

(vi)	Agriculture and food systems transformation, through financing, insurance and de-risking investment, youth and women entrepreneurship in agriculture, regional value chains, common agro-parks and agro-industrialization;

(b)	Provide technical assistance and advisory service to member States, the African Union Commission, regional economic communities and intergovernmental organizations to support sustainable development planning and policy formulation under the second 10-year implementation plan of Agenda 2063;

(c)	Convene peer-learning policy dialogues and regional consensus-building platforms and strengthen African universities and research institutions in knowledge generation in the above-mentioned areas.
18.74	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced African-led knowledge generation, peer-learning and consensus-building processes to advance climate-resilient development pathways;
(b)	Improved utilization of Africa’s mineral endowments to promote inclusive and sustainable development;
(c)	Development of policies, strategies and plans to enable the transition to environmentally sustainable economies;
(d)	Strengthened integration of climate resilience and disaster risk reduction approaches in national and regional planning;
(e)	Enhanced implementation by members of ECA of their nationally determined contributions and increased financing in this regard, including from the private sector;
(f)	Improved competitiveness, productivity, value addition and trade in agricultural goods and services;
(g)	Resilient, inclusive and nutrition-oriented food systems;
(h)	Inclusive land-based investments in agriculture and other natural resource sectors, in accordance with the African Union guiding principles for large-scale land-based investments in Africa;
(i)	Improved land tenure security, in accordance with the African Union framework and guidelines on land policy in Africa.
Programme performance in 2025
Members of ECA strengthen policy and institutional frameworks for climate-resilient agricultural value chains

18.75	Strengthening regional agricultural value chains and agro-industrial development is critical for improving agricultural productivity, expanding value addition, enhancing market connectivity, and advancing regional trade integration under the African Continental Free Trade Area. For countries such as Côte d’Ivoire and Ghana, where cocoa and rice are strategic commodities, having a coherent and evidence-based framework is essential to guide policy alignment and unlock regionally integrated agricultural growth. In 2025, the subprogramme, through its analytical work and policy advice, and in collaboration with subprogramme 7, component 1, enabled Côte d’Ivoire and Ghana to review and validate a policy framework aimed at strengthening regional agricultural value chains and agro-industrial parks. The two countries reviewed the framework designed to improve agricultural productivity, increase value addition, strengthen market linkages, and promote regional trade integration within the agricultural sector. Through these efforts, Côte d’Ivoire and Ghana aligned their policy and regulatory priorities towards the advancement of regional agricultural value chain development, ensuring that their national commitments reflect African Union guidelines and African Continental Free Trade Area objectives. This has reinforced the strategic conditions for coherent, regionally integrated and investment-ready agricultural value chains, enhancing both countries’ ability to drive sustained agricultural transformation and contribute more effectively to broader regional economic integration.
18.76	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.14).

children.text: Result 3: enhanced climate ambition and climate action in Africa – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.81	Africa is one of the lowest contributors to greenhouse gas emissions causing climate change, yet key development sectors have already experienced widespread losses and damages attributable to human-induced climate change, including biodiversity loss, water shortages, reduced food production and reduced economic growth. The subprogramme has been supporting countries in strengthening investment, policy, and institutional frameworks to advance climate-resilient development pathways. Its analytical and advisory support has improved national capacity to implement nationally determined contributions, integrate strategies to address the impacts of climate change into national development planning and apply climate-economic modelling in policymaking. It has also supported the creation of carbon markets and regional partnerships that expand knowledge exchange to advance Africa’s priorities on adaptation, loss and damage and climate finance under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change process.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.82	The lesson for the subprogramme was that, while regional workshops support peer learning and consensus-building, they are less effective in driving national policy adoption and investment decisions. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will deepen country-level engagement, expand demand-driven technical advisory services and applied climate-economic modelling, and work more closely with United Nations country teams and national institutions, while using regional platforms mainly for peer learning and validation. This will enable members of ECA to develop various institutional instruments such as policies, regulatory frameworks, planning tools, financial mechanisms and coordination platforms that are aligned with Africa’s priorities on addressing the impacts of climate change.
18.83	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.X).
Figure 18.X – Performance measure: number of institutional instruments developed to promote climate-resilient development pathways and address the impacts of climate change (cumulative)

children.text: Subprogramme 6 – Economic empowerment of women and youth
Objective

18.85	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to achieve the economic empowerment of women and youth in Africa, by strengthening the design and implementation of policies and programmes by members of ECA, in line with relevant global and regional commitments.
Strategy
18.86	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Undertake policy research, provide technical advice, and build capacity on issues relating to the economic empowerment of women and youth, with particular emphasis on financial inclusion, digitalization, education and entrepreneurship;

(b)	Provide technical support to members of ECA, including national machineries for gender and youth and relevant line ministries, on integrating a gender perspective and youth perspectives into development policies, programmes, frameworks and initiatives across different sectors and thematic areas of interest;

(c)	In collaboration with partners, roll out relevant equality, inclusion and empowerment indices and provide technical support to members of ECA in measuring progress and reporting on the status of implementation of global and regional commitments on gender equality, youth empowerment and social inclusion;

(d)	Develop the capacity of women and young entrepreneurs and women- and youth-owned micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises, in the area of digital skills, creative content, financial literacy, e-commerce and the use of digital tools, services and platforms as well as artificial intelligence.
18.87	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Strengthened integration of a gender perspective and youth perspectives into planning, budgeting, financing, and implementation of national policies and programmes;

(b)	Improved understanding of and response by policymakers to global and regional frameworks on gender equality and women’s empowerment and empowerment of youth, for the alignment of national development outcomes and the fulfilment of national commitments;

(c)	Increased opportunities for inclusion, greater economic empowerment and improved livelihoods for women and youth across Africa, through policies and tools designed to address existing and emerging barriers and inequalities;

(d)	Enhanced digital, financial and commercial access for women and youth by strengthening technological and fiscal policy frameworks and entrepreneurial ecosystems to promote their participation and economic empowerment.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened data collection, monitoring and reporting on gender equality and the empowerment of women

18.88	Significant gaps in the collection of sex-disaggregated data and gender statistics across Africa limit the ability of member States to effectively track progress towards national, regional and global commitments on gender equality and women’s empowerment. Coordination challenges between statistical offices, line ministries, and gender machineries further constrain the collection, integration and analysis of such data, undermining evidence-based policymaking and accountability. The Minimum Set of Gender Indicators for Africa provides guidance on the prioritization of indicators to be collected in Africa, acts as an important resource for the development of their own statistical frameworks and facilitates the harmonization of regional and sub-regional data collection. The subprogramme strengthened national capacities in Burundi and South Sudan for the collection of gender statistics. Through targeted technical assistance, capacity-building initiatives, and stakeholder consultations, these member States assessed their monitoring and reporting systems, identified key gaps to guide improvements and adopted the Minimum Set of Gender Indicators for Africa. This enabled improved coordination, strengthened data systems, and enabled more systematic, policy-relevant reporting on gender equality and the empowerment of women.
18.89	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.17).
Table 18.17 – Performance measure

children.text: Planned results for 2027
Result 1: members of ECA integrate a gender perspective into fiscal policy – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.90	The subprogramme’s work contributed to three members of ECA (Ethiopia, Kenya and Uganda) establishing working groups with mandates including integrating gender-sensitive budgeting approaches into national planning and budgeting systems, which did not meet the planned target of five member States establishing a mechanism to better integrate a gender perspective into their fiscal policies. The target was not met due to liquidity constraints, which hampered the delivery of planned activities in two additional countries.
18.91	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.18).

children.text: Result 2: members of ECA take action to improve the financial inclusion of women – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.92	The subprogramme’s work contributed to two members of ECA (the Gambia and Sierra Leone) conducting a situational analysis to identify constraints and opportunities for strengthening women’s financial inclusion, which met the planned target.

children.text: Result 3: members of ECA leverage digitalization to empower young entrepreneurs – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.94	Disparities in access to digital infrastructure, skills, finance and markets continue to limit the full participation of young entrepreneurs in economic opportunities offered by the digital economy. The subprogramme, through its knowledge products, highlighted the need to strengthen digital skills, ensure inclusive technological policies and foster entrepreneurial innovation among young people.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.95	The lesson for the subprogramme was the need to support members of ECA to collect more data and evidence to support policymaking on the empowerment and entrepreneurship of youth, with particular focus on the digital economy. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will provide technical assistance and capacity-building and promote knowledge-sharing, partnerships and coordination, to ensure that digital transformation translates into meaningful entrepreneurship opportunities for young people and sustainable economic growth.
18.96	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.20).

children.text: Subprogramme 7 – Subregional activities for development
Component 1 – Subregional activities in North Africa
Objective

18.98	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to advance inclusive growth and sustainable development in North Africa through strengthened economic diversification, fiscal management, development finance, regional integration and a better recognition of migrant workers’ economic contributions.
Strategy
18.99	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Develop evidence-based policies, strategies and reforms and provide technical assistance related to economic diversification, the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and development of resilient and inclusive economies, in partnership with ministries of trade and the African Union;
(b)	Provide advisory services and hold subregional consultations on the mobilization of domestic resources, fiscal and debt management and sustainable finance;

(c)	Provide technical assistance, advisory services and training for selected African countries on analysis, evidence-based policy formulation and developing adequate frameworks to mainstream and better leverage diasporas’ economic contributions;

(d)	Conduct research on challenges facing middle-income countries in North Africa, in particular those relating to development and climate finance, human capital and employment creation in North Africa, with a focus on strategic areas such as digitalization and the green economy in the context of sustainable development and poverty eradication.
18.100	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Adoption of evidence-based macroeconomic policies and structural reforms by members of ECA to improve debt management and fiscal space and enhance domestic resource mobilization, redirecting resource allocations towards sectors that have a high potential for inclusive and sustainable development;
(b)	Policies and strategies that foster regional integration, enhance productivity and create new jobs in higher-skilled and sustainable sectors;
(c)	Development of evidence-based policies and actions by members of ECA that leverage the contributions of diasporas to sustainable development.
18.112	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Provide knowledge, tools and policy recommendations on the acceleration of a demographic transition and related implications for sustainable development and climate resilience, including population dynamics, migration, health and mortality, ageing and living conditions, gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls, youth, and sustainable peace and resilience in West Africa, among others;

(b)	Engage in high-level policy dialogues and provide technical support and capacity-strengthening to countries on regional integration issues, including the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area, development planning, and the development of budgeting frameworks that are sensitive to a demographic dividend with special emphasis on the empowerment of women and young people.
18.113	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Demographic dividend dimensions mainstreamed in national planning, programming and budgeting processes;
(b)	Deepened regional economic and monetary integration through the operationalization of Vision 2050 of the Economic Community of West African States;
(c)	Accelerated implementation of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063 by member States of ECA and regional organizations, including enhanced capacities in development planning;
(d)	Accelerated implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area in Africa, with women and young people empowered to seize the opportunities of the Agreement.
18.125	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Provide technical assistance and advisory services to members and regional economic communities of ECA in Central Africa on:

(i)	Formulating and implementing national and subregional development plans and economic diversification strategies with a focus on intra-Central African trade and creating a business climate conducive to employment creation and resilient to external shocks;

(ii)	Translating their national and regional strategies for the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area into specific and bankable projects, using market access information and trade opportunity data, with a particular focus on the agricultural, energy and pharmaceutical sectors;
(iii)	Transforming transport corridors into development corridors, in line with the Central Africa consensual transport plan;

(iv)	Developing ICT infrastructure and regulatory frameworks required for the digital transition, including support for the establishment of technology innovation centres, in collaboration with the ECA Centre of Excellence on Digital Identity, Trade and Economy within subprogramme 3;

(v)	Harmonizing trade instruments of the Economic Community of Central African States and the Central African Economic and Monetary Community while developing a unified master plan for industrial development and economic diversification in Central Africa;
(b)	Collaborate with the private sector, subregional think tanks and regional economic communities in the above-mentioned areas;
(c)	Train members of ECA and United Nations country teams in the use of the ECA integrated planning and reporting toolkits.
18.126	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Strengthened regional value chains and industrial clusters, integrated with local economies, and expanded manufacturing and high-value service sectors;

(b)	Integration of the relevant economic diversification-related goals and targets of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063 into national development strategies and plans, common country assessments and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks;
(c)	Increased productivity of local firms and economies, including adoption of new technologies;
(d)	Strengthened role of the private sector in economic diversification in Central Africa.
18.138	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Provide support to countries and regional economic communities, through knowledge production, technical assistance, capacity-building and policy dialogues, on the development and effective implementation of their national and regional strategies for the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area, and the negotiation and arrangements concerning outstanding protocols and instruments on the Area;

(b)	Enable the private sector to take part in operationalizing the African Continental Free Trade Area, through structured capacity-building interventions that include detailed step-by-step guides, practical advisory services, and information sessions on key instruments and protocols;

(c)	Engage in policy dialogue and provide training to promote transboundary investments and cost-effective trade logistics, including the creation and use of digital platforms and commodity exchanges to reduce asymmetries of information;

(d)	Foster the development of regional value chains in food and agriculture through value chain analyses and the dissemination of information to public and private sector stakeholders about investment opportunities;

(e)	Support members of ECA to assess the socioeconomic potential of the blue economy and conduct market analyses for small and medium-sized enterprises, while keeping in mind the need to protect natural resources, and to operationalize, at the national level, the Africa Blue Economy Strategy and related strategies of regional economic communities;

(f)	Support members of ECA in the development of national tourism strategies, in line with the recommendations set out in the African tourism strategy, and provide training on the production of high-quality tourism statistics in the subregion.
18.139	The above-mentioned work will help members of ECA to make progress towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 5, 7, 8, 11, 12, 13, 14 and 17.
18.140	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	National development planning frameworks incorporating and amplifying blue economy policies and strategies;
(b)	Standardized methodologies for tourism data collection and analysis in the subregion;
(c)	Increased market diversification and economic resilience to external shocks, related in particular to the blue economy, the tourism sector and food-related value chains;
(d)	Deeper regional integration and improved connectivity of landlocked countries;
(e)	Higher levels of intraregional trade in goods and services;
(f)	Harmonization of investment regimes and investment promotion strategies, and improved regulatory frameworks governing investment.
18.152	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Undertake policy research and analysis on inclusive and sustainable industrialization, value chain development, regional integration and trade, private sector development, and poverty and inequality, provide technical assistance to member States, regional economic communities and intergovernmental organizations and disseminate best practices at the subregional and national levels in Southern Africa;

(b)	Develop and disseminate policy tools and guidelines for the promotion of trade, inclusive and sustainable industrialization, including value addition, manufacturing and value chain development, at the national and subregional levels in Southern Africa;

(c)	Support members of ECA in the implementation of Vision 2050 of the Southern African Development Community (SADC) and its regional indicative strategic development plan for 2020–2030, thereby contributing to the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 1, 8, 9 and 10 and related goals of Agenda 2063;

(d)	Collaborate with partners, including the African Union Southern Africa Regional Office, SADC, the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA), the African Development Bank, the African Export-Import Bank, the Arab Bank for Economic Development in Africa, private sector associations and research institutions:

(i)	To strengthen the capacities of members of ECA to advance inclusive and sustainable industrialization and regional integration through implementation of the SADC industrialization strategy and road map (2015–2063), the SADC Regional Energy Access Strategy and Action Plan (2020–2030), the COMESA industrial policy (2015–2030), the African Continental Free Trade Area and the free trade areas of the regional economic communities;

(ii)	To provide technical support for the development of strategies and guidelines for the integration of considerations related to climate change into industrialization and trade policies in Southern Africa;
(e)	Build the capacities of members of ECA, regional economic communities and the private sector:

(i)	To address the challenges and opportunities of the green economy in the context of sustainable development and poverty eradication, and to increase productive capacities, given the situation of energy resource deficiency in the region and the impact of climate change on key sectors;
(ii)	To address the impact of external shocks, by rolling out initiatives aimed at supporting their recovery and strengthening their resilience to future shocks;
(iii)	To leverage digitalization, science, technology and innovation to facilitate trade, growth, business development and enhanced productivity and competitiveness.
18.153	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced implementation of the SADC Vision 2050 and indicative strategic plan, the SADC industrialization strategy and road map and the COMESA industrial policy;

(b)	Economic diversification and transformation through value addition and the development of sustainable regional value chains, accelerated by transboundary industrial clusters, hubs and special economic zones in selected members of ECA;
(c)	Harmonization of national industrial policies with regional industrial frameworks and integration of considerations related to the environment and sustainability;

(d)	Accelerated implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and the free trade agreements of the regional economic communities, increased intraregional trade in goods and services and enhanced regional integration in Southern Africa;
(e)	Increased resilience and competitiveness of the private sector and micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises and enhanced participation in industry and trade in Southern Africa;
(f)	Evidence-based policy responses related to poverty, inequality and vulnerability situations in Southern Africa through informed analyses of trends and causal factors.

18.111	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to advance inclusive development in West Africa by strengthening countries’ capacity to integrate challenges related to demographic dynamics into their policies and planning processes and achieve regional integration.
Strategy
18.99	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Develop evidence-based policies, strategies and reforms and provide technical assistance related to economic diversification, the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and development of resilient and inclusive economies, in partnership with ministries of trade and the African Union;
