children.text: Somalia launches Centennial Vision 2060 and ratifies the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area

18.141	The Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area is a landmark agreement to boost intra-African trade and economic integration, while Somalia’s Centennial Vision 2060 outlines the country’s long-term development aspirations. The component advanced regional integration in Eastern Africa by supporting Somalia’s operationalization of the African Continental Free Trade Area and the development of its Centennial Vision 2060. Building on the validation of Somalia’s African Continental Free Trade Area National Implementation Strategy in November 2023, the component delivered targeted technical assistance and policy advice throughout 2024 and 2025, towards the alignment of trade priorities with long-term development objectives. These efforts strengthened institutional capacity and consensus on trade reforms, leading to Somalia’s historic accession to the East African Community in March 2024, the presidential launch of the Centennial Vision 2060 in June 2025, and the ratification of the Agreement by the Somali parliament in October 2025, providing a transformative framework for sustainable growth and regional integration.

children.text: Accelerated intraregional trade and investment in the SADC region through digital technology

18.154	Innovation and technology have potential to enable the private sector, including micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises, to improve their competitiveness and expand their national, regional and international market reach. The component provided technical assistance and advice to the SADC Business Council towards the development of the SADC Online Trade, Investment and Technology Platform, a digital portal focused on facilitating trade, investment and technology utilization for the private sector, and particularly such enterprises, in the SADC region. The Platform, which is hosted by the SADC Business Council, was developed to enhance the value and quality of trade and investment within the SADC region in a way that contributes to the development of local and regional economies and helps raise living standards. The Platform was officially launched and operationalized in 2025 as a digital tool for use by the private sector, particularly micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises in the subregion, in facilitating networking among importers and exporters; providing access to trade and market information, inputs, business support services, finance and technology; and serving as a gateway to other business portals and to the wider African Continental Free Trade Area market.

children.text: Result 2: members of ECA accelerate inclusive and sustainable growth through increased productivity, diversification and value addition – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.105	The component’s work contributed to increased knowledge materials available to member States on fostering inclusive and sustainable growth through support to small and medium-sized enterprise development, and on overcoming the “middle-income trap”, which did not meet the planned target of one additional policy measure adopted to promote productivity, diversification and value addition. The target was not met due to the need for additional time for the member State to develop and adopt such a policy measure.

Figure 18.XI – Performance measure: number of policy measures adopted by member States to promote increased productivity, diversification and value addition (cumulative)
Result 3: members of ECA take actions to maximize diaspora engagement as alternative sources of finance for development – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.107	Since the adoption of the Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration, members of ECA have intensified efforts to maximize the economic contribution of migration to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals. Since 2024, the component has supported members of ECA in implementing specific objectives (19, 20 and 23) of the Global Compact to develop evidence-based policies and actions that leverage migrants’ economic contributions. The component provided technical support to six members of ECA to formally integrate remittances and migrants’ positive contributions into national development plans and to develop adequate actions that help to channel remittances to productive investments and engage the diaspora as development actors. Furthermore, the component has supported members of ECA to better optimize domestic resources and mobilize sustainable finance for development.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.108	The lesson for the component was the need to further support members of ECA to unlock the potential of the positive contributions of their diaspora, by channelling remittances to investments. In applying the lesson, the component will provide technical assistance and policy advice to support countries in developing tools to maximize the economic contributions of the diaspora, optimize domestic resources and increase sustainable finance for development. In doing so, the component will reinforce internal collaboration with United Nations country teams and international organizations at the country level.
18.109	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XII).

18.121	The lesson for the component was that progress achieved through technical support is difficult to sustain in contexts affected by political instability and high staff turnover, which weaken institutional memory and disrupt the consistent application of reforms related to budgeting sensitive to the demographic dividend. In applying this lesson, the subprogramme will adopt a more continuous and system-focused approach by strengthening the operationalization of budgeting sensitive to the demographic dividend through a regional pool of multidisciplinary experts to ensure ongoing technical guidance, knowledge transfer, and national ownership. The component will also support the upgrade of budgetary information systems and introduce the application of budget transformation techniques and related automated analysis. In addition, the component will further enhance the regional digital monitoring platform to improve the sustainability and efficiency of evidence-based monitoring of progress on the African Union road map on harnessing the demographic dividend.
18.122	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XV).

18.134	The lesson for the component was that continued support is required, focusing on establishing or reinforcing formal national coordination mechanisms with clear mandates, defined membership and regular operating arrangements, to enhance policy coherence, improve inter-agency collaboration and ensure structured engagement with private sector stakeholders. In applying the lesson, the component will support the establishment or formal strengthening of national trade facilitation coordination mechanisms; facilitate structured public-private dialogue platforms aligned with regional integration priorities; promote greater clarity and coordination of mandates among trade, customs, industry and related authorities; and encourage peer learning and exchange of good practices among member States. This institutional focus complements other result areas by strengthening the governance foundations for sustainable regional economic integration.
18.135	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XVII).

18.148	The lesson for the component was that support for the development of regional strategies alone are insufficient. For such strategies to translate into tangible impacts, support to facilitate practical coordination and harmonized approaches is required. In applying the lesson, the component will support member States and subregional organizations to strengthen policy frameworks and enhance coordination to promote connectivity. In addition, the component will promote the use of digital platforms, such as e-commerce platforms, with a view to enhancing trade efficiency.
18.149	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.32).

18.161	The lesson for the component was the need to strengthen the awareness and knowledge of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area with its related protocols, and other trade agreements, among private sector actors and to address the constraints that limit effective engagement in regional and international trade. In applying the lesson, the component will develop targeted interventions and provide tailored technical support aimed at addressing constraints limiting enterprises, particularly micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises, from participating in regional and international trade. These will focus on improving adherence to non-tariff measures and enhancing such enterprises’ capacities to participate in export-oriented regional value chains and to serve as reliable input suppliers to large enterprises.
18.162	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XXI).
Figure 18.XII – Performance measure: number of tools developed by members of ECA to mobilize alternative source for financing development (cumulative)
Result 1: members of ECA assess debt sustainability and develop longer-term fiscal strategies – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

The implementation of the strategic road map led to the adoption, in 2026, of the Finance Act 2026, which introduced new regulations related to transfer pricing.

Result 3: enhanced national budgeting and monitoring systems to harness the demographic dividend – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.120	Harnessing the demographic dividend remains essential for strengthening sustainable development outcomes across members of ECA, as it links population dynamics to evidence-based planning and budgeting. The subprogramme has supported countries in applying the Budgeting Sensitive to the Demographic Dividend approach and related tools, building national capacities to integrate demographic considerations into planning and expenditure frameworks, and contributing to progress towards more demographic-dividend-responsive policies and budget processes.

Result 1: members of ECA develop special economic zones for sustainable and inclusive economic diversification – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.129	The component’s work contributed to the completion of pre-feasibility studies for a project on valorization of wood residues from forestry operations in Bertoua and Yokadouma in Cameroon, with a view to developing a new-generation special economic zone, which did not meet the planned target of two member States of ECA concluding pre-feasibility studies. The target was not met due to the political context in targeted countries, where ongoing electoral processes temporarily shifted focus from technical and economic initiatives.
18.130	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.28).

children.text: Result 2: Central African members of ECA enhance natural asset valuation and capitalization – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.131	The component’s work contributed to two additional countries (Congo and Sao Tome and Principe) being better equipped to independently apply natural capital accounting methods to conduct economic impact assessments of natural assets, for a cumulative total of four countries, which exceeded the cumulative planned target of three countries.
18.132	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XVI).
Figure 18.XVI – Performance measure: number of countries better equipped to independently apply natural capital accounting methods to conduct economic impact assessment of natural assets (cumulative)
Result 3: strengthened trade facilitation systems and institutional capacities to accelerate regional economic integration

18.133	Advancing regional economic integration in Central Africa requires not only the adoption of trade facilitation rules and regional commitments, but also effective institutional arrangements to implement them. While members of ECA have subscribed to regional and continental trade frameworks, implementation remains uneven due to fragmented inter-agency coordination, weakly institutionalized public-private dialogue and the absence of formal governance platforms. The absence of formalized and institutionalized coordination platforms weakens the implementation of trade facilitation measures and constrains meaningful private sector participation in regional integration processes. Experience has shown that, even where national strategies explicitly reference African Continental Free Trade Area implementation and regional value chain development, weak or ad hoc institutional dialogue limits operational follow-through and inter-agency coherence.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.108	The lesson for the component was the need to further support members of ECA to unlock the potential of the positive contributions of their diaspora, by channelling remittances to investments. In applying the lesson, the component will provide technical assistance and policy advice to support countries in developing tools to maximize the economic contributions of the diaspora, optimize domestic resources and increase sustainable finance for development. In doing so, the component will reinforce internal collaboration with United Nations country teams and international organizations at the country level.
18.109	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XII).

18.121	The lesson for the component was that progress achieved through technical support is difficult to sustain in contexts affected by political instability and high staff turnover, which weaken institutional memory and disrupt the consistent application of reforms related to budgeting sensitive to the demographic dividend. In applying this lesson, the subprogramme will adopt a more continuous and system-focused approach by strengthening the operationalization of budgeting sensitive to the demographic dividend through a regional pool of multidisciplinary experts to ensure ongoing technical guidance, knowledge transfer, and national ownership. The component will also support the upgrade of budgetary information systems and introduce the application of budget transformation techniques and related automated analysis. In addition, the component will further enhance the regional digital monitoring platform to improve the sustainability and efficiency of evidence-based monitoring of progress on the African Union road map on harnessing the demographic dividend.
18.122	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XV).

18.134	The lesson for the component was that continued support is required, focusing on establishing or reinforcing formal national coordination mechanisms with clear mandates, defined membership and regular operating arrangements, to enhance policy coherence, improve inter-agency collaboration and ensure structured engagement with private sector stakeholders. In applying the lesson, the component will support the establishment or formal strengthening of national trade facilitation coordination mechanisms; facilitate structured public-private dialogue platforms aligned with regional integration priorities; promote greater clarity and coordination of mandates among trade, customs, industry and related authorities; and encourage peer learning and exchange of good practices among member States. This institutional focus complements other result areas by strengthening the governance foundations for sustainable regional economic integration.
18.135	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XVII).

18.148	The lesson for the component was that support for the development of regional strategies alone are insufficient. For such strategies to translate into tangible impacts, support to facilitate practical coordination and harmonized approaches is required. In applying the lesson, the component will support member States and subregional organizations to strengthen policy frameworks and enhance coordination to promote connectivity. In addition, the component will promote the use of digital platforms, such as e-commerce platforms, with a view to enhancing trade efficiency.
18.149	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.32).

18.161	The lesson for the component was the need to strengthen the awareness and knowledge of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area with its related protocols, and other trade agreements, among private sector actors and to address the constraints that limit effective engagement in regional and international trade. In applying the lesson, the component will develop targeted interventions and provide tailored technical support aimed at addressing constraints limiting enterprises, particularly micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises, from participating in regional and international trade. These will focus on improving adherence to non-tariff measures and enhancing such enterprises’ capacities to participate in export-oriented regional value chains and to serve as reliable input suppliers to large enterprises.
18.162	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XXI).
Figure 18.XVII – Performance measure: number of members of ECA in Central Africa that formalized trade facilitation and coordination mechanisms (cumulative)
Result 1: members of ECA and regional organizations develop investment promotion strategies – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.143	The component’s work contributed to two regional organizations (Central Corridor Transit Transport Facilitation Agency and Northern Corridor Transit and Transport Coordination Authority) developing inland waterways transport strategies aimed at attracting greater investment in regional transport infrastructure, which did not meet the planned target of one ECA member State developing a national investment promotion strategy. The target was not met, as the component’s focus was placed on completing regional strategies prior to the initiation of national-level plans.

Result 3: strengthened connectivity and infrastructure for deeper regional integration – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.147	Diversified and efficient connectivity is crucial for regional integration and for achieving Sustainable Development Goals 2 and 9. Landlocked countries in Eastern Africa remain dependent on limited corridors and transport modes, increasing costs and vulnerability to disruptions. To lay the foundation for integrated connectivity and trade systems, the component supported member States in Eastern Africa to develop inland waterways strategies, harmonize regional policies for inland waterway transport, and promote digital trade initiatives to improve food security.

children.text: Result 1: accelerating the transition towards more sustainable economies in Southern Africa – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.156	The component’s work contributed to one policy intervention, the SADC Just Energy Transition Policy Framework, developed by the regional economic community, which did not meet the planned target of developing two additional policy interventions. The target was not met due to the delay in finalizing interventions and to allow more time for the parties in Southern Africa to conclude negotiations on the outstanding terms of the planned policy intervention.
18.157	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XIX).

Figure 18.XIX – Performance measure: number of policy interventions developed by countries and regional economic communities in Southern Africa to support the transition towards more sustainable economies (cumulative)
Result 2: inclusive industrialization through value chain development in Southern Africa – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.158	The component’s work contributed to initiating engagement with Southern African countries on developing policy frameworks on value chain development, which did not meet the planned target of one additional Southern African country developing a policy framework. The target was not met because the component could not provide planned technical and advisory services or organize planned stakeholder consultative meetings due to liquidity constraints.
18.159	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XX).
Figure 18.XX – Performance measure: number of Southern African countries that develop policy frameworks on value chain development (cumulative)
Result 3: accelerated implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and other trade agreements in Southern Africa – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.160	The African Continental Free Trade Area aims to catalyse the structural transformation of African economies by promoting industrialization, trade and regional integration, thereby creating decent jobs and reducing poverty and inequality. The African Continental Free Trade Area has the potential to develop regional value chains and expand market access and investment opportunities for both micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises and large businesses. However, a critical factor for success lies with the effective implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and other related trade agreements. The component has been providing technical support to countries in developing national African Continental Free Trade Area strategies to guide effective implementation of the Agreement. The component has also produced knowledge products and has delivered capacity-building workshops for micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises on trading under the African Continental Free Trade Area.

children.text: Objective

18.98	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to advance inclusive growth and sustainable development in North Africa through strengthened economic diversification, fiscal management, development finance, regional integration and a better recognition of migrant workers’ economic contributions.
Strategy
18.99	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Develop evidence-based policies, strategies and reforms and provide technical assistance related to economic diversification, the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and development of resilient and inclusive economies, in partnership with ministries of trade and the African Union;
(b)	Provide advisory services and hold subregional consultations on the mobilization of domestic resources, fiscal and debt management and sustainable finance;

(c)	Provide technical assistance, advisory services and training for selected African countries on analysis, evidence-based policy formulation and developing adequate frameworks to mainstream and better leverage diasporas’ economic contributions;

(d)	Conduct research on challenges facing middle-income countries in North Africa, in particular those relating to development and climate finance, human capital and employment creation in North Africa, with a focus on strategic areas such as digitalization and the green economy in the context of sustainable development and poverty eradication.
18.100	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Adoption of evidence-based macroeconomic policies and structural reforms by members of ECA to improve debt management and fiscal space and enhance domestic resource mobilization, redirecting resource allocations towards sectors that have a high potential for inclusive and sustainable development;
(b)	Policies and strategies that foster regional integration, enhance productivity and create new jobs in higher-skilled and sustainable sectors;
(c)	Development of evidence-based policies and actions by members of ECA that leverage the contributions of diasporas to sustainable development.
18.112	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Provide knowledge, tools and policy recommendations on the acceleration of a demographic transition and related implications for sustainable development and climate resilience, including population dynamics, migration, health and mortality, ageing and living conditions, gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls, youth, and sustainable peace and resilience in West Africa, among others;

(b)	Engage in high-level policy dialogues and provide technical support and capacity-strengthening to countries on regional integration issues, including the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area, development planning, and the development of budgeting frameworks that are sensitive to a demographic dividend with special emphasis on the empowerment of women and young people.
18.113	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Demographic dividend dimensions mainstreamed in national planning, programming and budgeting processes;
(b)	Deepened regional economic and monetary integration through the operationalization of Vision 2050 of the Economic Community of West African States;
(c)	Accelerated implementation of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063 by member States of ECA and regional organizations, including enhanced capacities in development planning;
(d)	Accelerated implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area in Africa, with women and young people empowered to seize the opportunities of the Agreement.
18.125	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Provide technical assistance and advisory services to members and regional economic communities of ECA in Central Africa on:

(i)	Formulating and implementing national and subregional development plans and economic diversification strategies with a focus on intra-Central African trade and creating a business climate conducive to employment creation and resilient to external shocks;

(ii)	Translating their national and regional strategies for the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area into specific and bankable projects, using market access information and trade opportunity data, with a particular focus on the agricultural, energy and pharmaceutical sectors;
(iii)	Transforming transport corridors into development corridors, in line with the Central Africa consensual transport plan;

(iv)	Developing ICT infrastructure and regulatory frameworks required for the digital transition, including support for the establishment of technology innovation centres, in collaboration with the ECA Centre of Excellence on Digital Identity, Trade and Economy within subprogramme 3;

(v)	Harmonizing trade instruments of the Economic Community of Central African States and the Central African Economic and Monetary Community while developing a unified master plan for industrial development and economic diversification in Central Africa;
(b)	Collaborate with the private sector, subregional think tanks and regional economic communities in the above-mentioned areas;
(c)	Train members of ECA and United Nations country teams in the use of the ECA integrated planning and reporting toolkits.
18.126	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Strengthened regional value chains and industrial clusters, integrated with local economies, and expanded manufacturing and high-value service sectors;

(b)	Integration of the relevant economic diversification-related goals and targets of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063 into national development strategies and plans, common country assessments and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks;
(c)	Increased productivity of local firms and economies, including adoption of new technologies;
(d)	Strengthened role of the private sector in economic diversification in Central Africa.
18.138	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Provide support to countries and regional economic communities, through knowledge production, technical assistance, capacity-building and policy dialogues, on the development and effective implementation of their national and regional strategies for the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area, and the negotiation and arrangements concerning outstanding protocols and instruments on the Area;

(b)	Enable the private sector to take part in operationalizing the African Continental Free Trade Area, through structured capacity-building interventions that include detailed step-by-step guides, practical advisory services, and information sessions on key instruments and protocols;

(c)	Engage in policy dialogue and provide training to promote transboundary investments and cost-effective trade logistics, including the creation and use of digital platforms and commodity exchanges to reduce asymmetries of information;

(d)	Foster the development of regional value chains in food and agriculture through value chain analyses and the dissemination of information to public and private sector stakeholders about investment opportunities;

(e)	Support members of ECA to assess the socioeconomic potential of the blue economy and conduct market analyses for small and medium-sized enterprises, while keeping in mind the need to protect natural resources, and to operationalize, at the national level, the Africa Blue Economy Strategy and related strategies of regional economic communities;

(f)	Support members of ECA in the development of national tourism strategies, in line with the recommendations set out in the African tourism strategy, and provide training on the production of high-quality tourism statistics in the subregion.
18.139	The above-mentioned work will help members of ECA to make progress towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 5, 7, 8, 11, 12, 13, 14 and 17.
18.140	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	National development planning frameworks incorporating and amplifying blue economy policies and strategies;
(b)	Standardized methodologies for tourism data collection and analysis in the subregion;
(c)	Increased market diversification and economic resilience to external shocks, related in particular to the blue economy, the tourism sector and food-related value chains;
(d)	Deeper regional integration and improved connectivity of landlocked countries;
(e)	Higher levels of intraregional trade in goods and services;
(f)	Harmonization of investment regimes and investment promotion strategies, and improved regulatory frameworks governing investment.
18.152	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Undertake policy research and analysis on inclusive and sustainable industrialization, value chain development, regional integration and trade, private sector development, and poverty and inequality, provide technical assistance to member States, regional economic communities and intergovernmental organizations and disseminate best practices at the subregional and national levels in Southern Africa;

(b)	Develop and disseminate policy tools and guidelines for the promotion of trade, inclusive and sustainable industrialization, including value addition, manufacturing and value chain development, at the national and subregional levels in Southern Africa;

(c)	Support members of ECA in the implementation of Vision 2050 of the Southern African Development Community (SADC) and its regional indicative strategic development plan for 2020–2030, thereby contributing to the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 1, 8, 9 and 10 and related goals of Agenda 2063;

(d)	Collaborate with partners, including the African Union Southern Africa Regional Office, SADC, the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA), the African Development Bank, the African Export-Import Bank, the Arab Bank for Economic Development in Africa, private sector associations and research institutions:

(i)	To strengthen the capacities of members of ECA to advance inclusive and sustainable industrialization and regional integration through implementation of the SADC industrialization strategy and road map (2015–2063), the SADC Regional Energy Access Strategy and Action Plan (2020–2030), the COMESA industrial policy (2015–2030), the African Continental Free Trade Area and the free trade areas of the regional economic communities;

(ii)	To provide technical support for the development of strategies and guidelines for the integration of considerations related to climate change into industrialization and trade policies in Southern Africa;
(e)	Build the capacities of members of ECA, regional economic communities and the private sector:

(i)	To address the challenges and opportunities of the green economy in the context of sustainable development and poverty eradication, and to increase productive capacities, given the situation of energy resource deficiency in the region and the impact of climate change on key sectors;
(ii)	To address the impact of external shocks, by rolling out initiatives aimed at supporting their recovery and strengthening their resilience to future shocks;
(iii)	To leverage digitalization, science, technology and innovation to facilitate trade, growth, business development and enhanced productivity and competitiveness.
18.153	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced implementation of the SADC Vision 2050 and indicative strategic plan, the SADC industrialization strategy and road map and the COMESA industrial policy;

(b)	Economic diversification and transformation through value addition and the development of sustainable regional value chains, accelerated by transboundary industrial clusters, hubs and special economic zones in selected members of ECA;
(c)	Harmonization of national industrial policies with regional industrial frameworks and integration of considerations related to the environment and sustainability;

(d)	Accelerated implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and the free trade agreements of the regional economic communities, increased intraregional trade in goods and services and enhanced regional integration in Southern Africa;
(e)	Increased resilience and competitiveness of the private sector and micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises and enhanced participation in industry and trade in Southern Africa;
(f)	Evidence-based policy responses related to poverty, inequality and vulnerability situations in Southern Africa through informed analyses of trends and causal factors.

18.111	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to advance inclusive development in West Africa by strengthening countries’ capacity to integrate challenges related to demographic dynamics into their policies and planning processes and achieve regional integration.

18.138	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Provide support to countries and regional economic communities, through knowledge production, technical assistance, capacity-building and policy dialogues, on the development and effective implementation of their national and regional strategies for the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area, and the negotiation and arrangements concerning outstanding protocols and instruments on the Area;

(b)	Enable the private sector to take part in operationalizing the African Continental Free Trade Area, through structured capacity-building interventions that include detailed step-by-step guides, practical advisory services, and information sessions on key instruments and protocols;

(c)	Engage in policy dialogue and provide training to promote transboundary investments and cost-effective trade logistics, including the creation and use of digital platforms and commodity exchanges to reduce asymmetries of information;

(d)	Foster the development of regional value chains in food and agriculture through value chain analyses and the dissemination of information to public and private sector stakeholders about investment opportunities;

(e)	Support members of ECA to assess the socioeconomic potential of the blue economy and conduct market analyses for small and medium-sized enterprises, while keeping in mind the need to protect natural resources, and to operationalize, at the national level, the Africa Blue Economy Strategy and related strategies of regional economic communities;

(f)	Support members of ECA in the development of national tourism strategies, in line with the recommendations set out in the African tourism strategy, and provide training on the production of high-quality tourism statistics in the subregion.
18.139	The above-mentioned work will help members of ECA to make progress towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 5, 7, 8, 11, 12, 13, 14 and 17.
18.140	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	National development planning frameworks incorporating and amplifying blue economy policies and strategies;
(b)	Standardized methodologies for tourism data collection and analysis in the subregion;
(c)	Increased market diversification and economic resilience to external shocks, related in particular to the blue economy, the tourism sector and food-related value chains;
(d)	Deeper regional integration and improved connectivity of landlocked countries;
(e)	Higher levels of intraregional trade in goods and services;
(f)	Harmonization of investment regimes and investment promotion strategies, and improved regulatory frameworks governing investment.
18.152	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Undertake policy research and analysis on inclusive and sustainable industrialization, value chain development, regional integration and trade, private sector development, and poverty and inequality, provide technical assistance to member States, regional economic communities and intergovernmental organizations and disseminate best practices at the subregional and national levels in Southern Africa;

(b)	Develop and disseminate policy tools and guidelines for the promotion of trade, inclusive and sustainable industrialization, including value addition, manufacturing and value chain development, at the national and subregional levels in Southern Africa;

(c)	Support members of ECA in the implementation of Vision 2050 of the Southern African Development Community (SADC) and its regional indicative strategic development plan for 2020–2030, thereby contributing to the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 1, 8, 9 and 10 and related goals of Agenda 2063;

(d)	Collaborate with partners, including the African Union Southern Africa Regional Office, SADC, the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA), the African Development Bank, the African Export-Import Bank, the Arab Bank for Economic Development in Africa, private sector associations and research institutions:

(i)	To strengthen the capacities of members of ECA to advance inclusive and sustainable industrialization and regional integration through implementation of the SADC industrialization strategy and road map (2015–2063), the SADC Regional Energy Access Strategy and Action Plan (2020–2030), the COMESA industrial policy (2015–2030), the African Continental Free Trade Area and the free trade areas of the regional economic communities;

(ii)	To provide technical support for the development of strategies and guidelines for the integration of considerations related to climate change into industrialization and trade policies in Southern Africa;
(e)	Build the capacities of members of ECA, regional economic communities and the private sector:

(i)	To address the challenges and opportunities of the green economy in the context of sustainable development and poverty eradication, and to increase productive capacities, given the situation of energy resource deficiency in the region and the impact of climate change on key sectors;
(ii)	To address the impact of external shocks, by rolling out initiatives aimed at supporting their recovery and strengthening their resilience to future shocks;
(iii)	To leverage digitalization, science, technology and innovation to facilitate trade, growth, business development and enhanced productivity and competitiveness.
18.153	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced implementation of the SADC Vision 2050 and indicative strategic plan, the SADC industrialization strategy and road map and the COMESA industrial policy;

(b)	Economic diversification and transformation through value addition and the development of sustainable regional value chains, accelerated by transboundary industrial clusters, hubs and special economic zones in selected members of ECA;
(c)	Harmonization of national industrial policies with regional industrial frameworks and integration of considerations related to the environment and sustainability;

(d)	Accelerated implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and the free trade agreements of the regional economic communities, increased intraregional trade in goods and services and enhanced regional integration in Southern Africa;
(e)	Increased resilience and competitiveness of the private sector and micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises and enhanced participation in industry and trade in Southern Africa;
(f)	Evidence-based policy responses related to poverty, inequality and vulnerability situations in Southern Africa through informed analyses of trends and causal factors.

18.137	The objective, to which this component contributes, is to achieve deeper regional integration in Eastern Africa by advancing the operationalization of the African Continental Free Trade Area, increasing intraregional investments, harnessing the blue economy and enhancing regional tourism.
Strategy
18.99	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Develop evidence-based policies, strategies and reforms and provide technical assistance related to economic diversification, the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and development of resilient and inclusive economies, in partnership with ministries of trade and the African Union;
(b)	Provide advisory services and hold subregional consultations on the mobilization of domestic resources, fiscal and debt management and sustainable finance;

(c)	Provide technical assistance, advisory services and training for selected African countries on analysis, evidence-based policy formulation and developing adequate frameworks to mainstream and better leverage diasporas’ economic contributions;

(d)	Conduct research on challenges facing middle-income countries in North Africa, in particular those relating to development and climate finance, human capital and employment creation in North Africa, with a focus on strategic areas such as digitalization and the green economy in the context of sustainable development and poverty eradication.
18.100	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Adoption of evidence-based macroeconomic policies and structural reforms by members of ECA to improve debt management and fiscal space and enhance domestic resource mobilization, redirecting resource allocations towards sectors that have a high potential for inclusive and sustainable development;
(b)	Policies and strategies that foster regional integration, enhance productivity and create new jobs in higher-skilled and sustainable sectors;
(c)	Development of evidence-based policies and actions by members of ECA that leverage the contributions of diasporas to sustainable development.
18.112	To contribute to the objective, the component will:

(a)	Provide knowledge, tools and policy recommendations on the acceleration of a demographic transition and related implications for sustainable development and climate resilience, including population dynamics, migration, health and mortality, ageing and living conditions, gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls, youth, and sustainable peace and resilience in West Africa, among others;

(b)	Engage in high-level policy dialogues and provide technical support and capacity-strengthening to countries on regional integration issues, including the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area, development planning, and the development of budgeting frameworks that are sensitive to a demographic dividend with special emphasis on the empowerment of women and young people.
18.113	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Demographic dividend dimensions mainstreamed in national planning, programming and budgeting processes;
(b)	Deepened regional economic and monetary integration through the operationalization of Vision 2050 of the Economic Community of West African States;
(c)	Accelerated implementation of the 2030 Agenda and Agenda 2063 by member States of ECA and regional organizations, including enhanced capacities in development planning;
(d)	Accelerated implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area in Africa, with women and young people empowered to seize the opportunities of the Agreement.
18.125	To contribute to the objective, the component will:
(a)	Provide technical assistance and advisory services to members and regional economic communities of ECA in Central Africa on:

(i)	Formulating and implementing national and subregional development plans and economic diversification strategies with a focus on intra-Central African trade and creating a business climate conducive to employment creation and resilient to external shocks;

(ii)	Translating their national and regional strategies for the implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area into specific and bankable projects, using market access information and trade opportunity data, with a particular focus on the agricultural, energy and pharmaceutical sectors;
(iii)	Transforming transport corridors into development corridors, in line with the Central Africa consensual transport plan;

(iv)	Developing ICT infrastructure and regulatory frameworks required for the digital transition, including support for the establishment of technology innovation centres, in collaboration with the ECA Centre of Excellence on Digital Identity, Trade and Economy within subprogramme 3;

(v)	Harmonizing trade instruments of the Economic Community of Central African States and the Central African Economic and Monetary Community while developing a unified master plan for industrial development and economic diversification in Central Africa;
(b)	Collaborate with the private sector, subregional think tanks and regional economic communities in the above-mentioned areas;
(c)	Train members of ECA and United Nations country teams in the use of the ECA integrated planning and reporting toolkits.

Programme performance in 2025
Members of ECA take action to increase sustainable finance

18.101	Africa faces mounting external debt and escalating debt service obligations that are increasingly crowding out resources for essential public services and investments. The component contributed to strengthening the capacity of African countries to advance sustainable finance practices. Through a regional advocacy platform, the component promoted regional peer learning and policy dialogue for 12 African countries by presenting their experiences and exchanging best practices on how to leverage innovative financing instruments such as green bonds, sustainability-linked bonds, and debt-for-purpose swaps. At the national level, the component contributed to enhancing public debt management through sustained advisory services, technical assistance and capacity-building programmes. These efforts led to the establishment of a Climate Finance Unit within the Public Debt Management Division in Sierra Leone in 2024, to mobilize and manage climate finance for sustainable national growth; and to the development, in 2025, of a national medium-term debt strategy in Egypt, which aims to reduce the country’s debt reliance. The strategy was adopted in January 2026.
18.102	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.22).
Table 18.22 – Performance measure

table_content.text: 2023 (actual)
2024 (actual)
2025 (actual)
Sierra Leone established a Climate Finance Unit within the Public Debt Management Office
Egypt developed a medium-term debt strategy (2026–2029)

children.text: Members of ECA in West Africa integrate agricultural value chain analysis into planning frameworks

18.114	Developing competitive cross-border value chains is central to Africa’s regional integration agenda. By enabling specialization, scale, and value addition across borders, regional agricultural value chains can unlock higher productivity, increase export earnings, catalyse technology and skills transfer, create jobs and improve food security. In 2023, the component supported countries to conduct regional integration impact assessments, which enabled them to identify priority regional value chains to be promoted. In 2024, the component organized a training series for national experts on trade facilitation and regional integration, including integrating agriculture value chain analysis into planning frameworks. In 2025, the component, in collaboration with subprogramme 5, provided policy advice and technical assistance to Côte d’Ivoire and Ghana to identify policy, regulatory and institutional reforms needed to strengthen regional value chains integration under the African Continental Free Trade Area. It provided policymakers with evidence-based recommendations to reduce post-harvest losses, improve logistics and customs management, and enhance cross-border cooperation. Through this support, cross-border value chain analysis was integrated into national development planning processes of both countries.
18.115	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.25).
Table 18.25 – Performance measure

table_content.text: 2023 (actual)
2024 (actual)
2025 (actual)
15 Member States participated in regional integration impact assessments
Approximately 150 national and regional experts from 15 countries in West Africa strengthened their knowledge and capacities on trade facilitation and regional value chains
Côte d’Ivoire and Ghana integrated cross-border value chain analysis into national development planning processes

children.text: Sao Tome and Principe developed and validated its first tourism satellite accounts

18.127	Tourism is a vital sector for small island developing States, providing avenues for economic diversification and sustainable and inclusive growth. Tourism satellite accounts provide a standard statistical framework to measure the economic contribution of tourism to a national economy, thereby enhancing planning, policy formulation, and resource mobilization in support of sustainable development. The component supported Sao Tome and Principe to develop and validate its first tourism satellite accounts. The component provided technical assistance and held workshops with national stakeholders on strengthening natural capital accounting, conducting demand- and supply-side analysis, and high-level policy dialogues. These efforts culminated in the validation and official handover of the tourism satellite accounts to the national Government.
18.128	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.27).

Sao Tome and Principe validated its first tourism satellite accounts, enabling the country to measure the contribution of tourism to its gross domestic product

children.text: Somalia launches Centennial Vision 2060 and ratifies the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area

18.141	The Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area is a landmark agreement to boost intra-African trade and economic integration, while Somalia’s Centennial Vision 2060 outlines the country’s long-term development aspirations. The component advanced regional integration in Eastern Africa by supporting Somalia’s operationalization of the African Continental Free Trade Area and the development of its Centennial Vision 2060. Building on the validation of Somalia’s African Continental Free Trade Area National Implementation Strategy in November 2023, the component delivered targeted technical assistance and policy advice throughout 2024 and 2025, towards the alignment of trade priorities with long-term development objectives. These efforts strengthened institutional capacity and consensus on trade reforms, leading to Somalia’s historic accession to the East African Community in March 2024, the presidential launch of the Centennial Vision 2060 in June 2025, and the ratification of the Agreement by the Somali parliament in October 2025, providing a transformative framework for sustainable growth and regional integration.
18.142	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.30).

children.text: Accelerated intraregional trade and investment in the SADC region through digital technology

18.154	Innovation and technology have potential to enable the private sector, including micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises, to improve their competitiveness and expand their national, regional and international market reach. The component provided technical assistance and advice to the SADC Business Council towards the development of the SADC Online Trade, Investment and Technology Platform, a digital portal focused on facilitating trade, investment and technology utilization for the private sector, and particularly such enterprises, in the SADC region. The Platform, which is hosted by the SADC Business Council, was developed to enhance the value and quality of trade and investment within the SADC region in a way that contributes to the development of local and regional economies and helps raise living standards. The Platform was officially launched and operationalized in 2025 as a digital tool for use by the private sector, particularly micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises in the subregion, in facilitating networking among importers and exporters; providing access to trade and market information, inputs, business support services, finance and technology; and serving as a gateway to other business portals and to the wider African Continental Free Trade Area market.
18.155	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.34).

children.text: Result 2: members of ECA accelerate inclusive and sustainable growth through increased productivity, diversification and value addition – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

18.105	The component’s work contributed to increased knowledge materials available to member States on fostering inclusive and sustainable growth through support to small and medium-sized enterprise development, and on overcoming the “middle-income trap”, which did not meet the planned target of one additional policy measure adopted to promote productivity, diversification and value addition. The target was not met due to the need for additional time for the member State to develop and adopt such a policy measure.
18.106	Progress towards the objective and the target for 2027 are presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XI).
Figure 18.XI – Performance measure: number of policy measures adopted by member States to promote increased productivity, diversification and value addition (cumulative)
Result 3: members of ECA take actions to maximize diaspora engagement as alternative sources of finance for development – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.107	Since the adoption of the Global Compact for Safe, Orderly and Regular Migration, members of ECA have intensified efforts to maximize the economic contribution of migration to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals. Since 2024, the component has supported members of ECA in implementing specific objectives (19, 20 and 23) of the Global Compact to develop evidence-based policies and actions that leverage migrants’ economic contributions. The component provided technical support to six members of ECA to formally integrate remittances and migrants’ positive contributions into national development plans and to develop adequate actions that help to channel remittances to productive investments and engage the diaspora as development actors. Furthermore, the component has supported members of ECA to better optimize domestic resources and mobilize sustainable finance for development.
Lessons learned and planned change

18.108	The lesson for the component was the need to further support members of ECA to unlock the potential of the positive contributions of their diaspora, by channelling remittances to investments. In applying the lesson, the component will provide technical assistance and policy advice to support countries in developing tools to maximize the economic contributions of the diaspora, optimize domestic resources and increase sustainable finance for development. In doing so, the component will reinforce internal collaboration with United Nations country teams and international organizations at the country level.
18.109	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XII).

18.121	The lesson for the component was that progress achieved through technical support is difficult to sustain in contexts affected by political instability and high staff turnover, which weaken institutional memory and disrupt the consistent application of reforms related to budgeting sensitive to the demographic dividend. In applying this lesson, the subprogramme will adopt a more continuous and system-focused approach by strengthening the operationalization of budgeting sensitive to the demographic dividend through a regional pool of multidisciplinary experts to ensure ongoing technical guidance, knowledge transfer, and national ownership. The component will also support the upgrade of budgetary information systems and introduce the application of budget transformation techniques and related automated analysis. In addition, the component will further enhance the regional digital monitoring platform to improve the sustainability and efficiency of evidence-based monitoring of progress on the African Union road map on harnessing the demographic dividend.
18.122	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XV).

18.134	The lesson for the component was that continued support is required, focusing on establishing or reinforcing formal national coordination mechanisms with clear mandates, defined membership and regular operating arrangements, to enhance policy coherence, improve inter-agency collaboration and ensure structured engagement with private sector stakeholders. In applying the lesson, the component will support the establishment or formal strengthening of national trade facilitation coordination mechanisms; facilitate structured public-private dialogue platforms aligned with regional integration priorities; promote greater clarity and coordination of mandates among trade, customs, industry and related authorities; and encourage peer learning and exchange of good practices among member States. This institutional focus complements other result areas by strengthening the governance foundations for sustainable regional economic integration.
18.135	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XVII).

18.148	The lesson for the component was that support for the development of regional strategies alone are insufficient. For such strategies to translate into tangible impacts, support to facilitate practical coordination and harmonized approaches is required. In applying the lesson, the component will support member States and subregional organizations to strengthen policy frameworks and enhance coordination to promote connectivity. In addition, the component will promote the use of digital platforms, such as e-commerce platforms, with a view to enhancing trade efficiency.
18.149	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see table 18.32).

18.161	The lesson for the component was the need to strengthen the awareness and knowledge of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area with its related protocols, and other trade agreements, among private sector actors and to address the constraints that limit effective engagement in regional and international trade. In applying the lesson, the component will develop targeted interventions and provide tailored technical support aimed at addressing constraints limiting enterprises, particularly micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises, from participating in regional and international trade. These will focus on improving adherence to non-tariff measures and enhancing such enterprises’ capacities to participate in export-oriented regional value chains and to serve as reliable input suppliers to large enterprises.
18.162	Expected progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 18.XXI).
Figure 18.XII – Performance measure: number of tools developed by members of ECA to mobilize alternative source for financing development (cumulative)

The implementation of the strategic road map led to the adoption, in 2026, of the Finance Act 2026, which introduced new regulations related to transfer pricing.

Result 3: enhanced national budgeting and monitoring systems to harness the demographic dividend – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.120	Harnessing the demographic dividend remains essential for strengthening sustainable development outcomes across members of ECA, as it links population dynamics to evidence-based planning and budgeting. The subprogramme has supported countries in applying the Budgeting Sensitive to the Demographic Dividend approach and related tools, building national capacities to integrate demographic considerations into planning and expenditure frameworks, and contributing to progress towards more demographic-dividend-responsive policies and budget processes.

Result 3: strengthened trade facilitation systems and institutional capacities to accelerate regional economic integration

18.133	Advancing regional economic integration in Central Africa requires not only the adoption of trade facilitation rules and regional commitments, but also effective institutional arrangements to implement them. While members of ECA have subscribed to regional and continental trade frameworks, implementation remains uneven due to fragmented inter-agency coordination, weakly institutionalized public-private dialogue and the absence of formal governance platforms. The absence of formalized and institutionalized coordination platforms weakens the implementation of trade facilitation measures and constrains meaningful private sector participation in regional integration processes. Experience has shown that, even where national strategies explicitly reference African Continental Free Trade Area implementation and regional value chain development, weak or ad hoc institutional dialogue limits operational follow-through and inter-agency coherence.

Result 3: strengthened connectivity and infrastructure for deeper regional integration – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.147	Diversified and efficient connectivity is crucial for regional integration and for achieving Sustainable Development Goals 2 and 9. Landlocked countries in Eastern Africa remain dependent on limited corridors and transport modes, increasing costs and vulnerability to disruptions. To lay the foundation for integrated connectivity and trade systems, the component supported member States in Eastern Africa to develop inland waterways strategies, harmonize regional policies for inland waterway transport, and promote digital trade initiatives to improve food security.

Result 3: accelerated implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and other trade agreements in Southern Africa – Proposed programme plan for 2027

18.160	The African Continental Free Trade Area aims to catalyse the structural transformation of African economies by promoting industrialization, trade and regional integration, thereby creating decent jobs and reducing poverty and inequality. The African Continental Free Trade Area has the potential to develop regional value chains and expand market access and investment opportunities for both micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises and large businesses. However, a critical factor for success lies with the effective implementation of the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area and other related trade agreements. The component has been providing technical support to countries in developing national African Continental Free Trade Area strategies to guide effective implementation of the Agreement. The component has also produced knowledge products and has delivered capacity-building workshops for micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises on trading under the African Continental Free Trade Area.
